stories.title: 23-Year-Old Black Man Becomes First in Louisiana to be Cured of Sickle Cell Disease Through Gene Therapy
stories.summary: Daniel Cressy, 23, is the first person in Louisiana functionally cured of sickle cell disease through gene therapy. After a grueling two-year treatment using his own edited stem cells, doctors say the disease is no longer active. He now plans to launch a nonprofit to support others undergoing treatment.
stories.extended_summary: Doctors say the disease that once threatened Daniel Cressy's dream of becoming a pilot is no longer active in his body. The 23-year-old from New Orleans spent two years in a grueling gene therapy trial at Manning Family Children’s Hospital, where his own stem cells were edited to stop producing defective cells. Now functionally cured, Cressy is the first in Louisiana to achieve this — and he’s turning his experience into a nonprofit to fight the loneliness and uncertainty that sickle cell patients face.
stories.headline_split: 23-Year-Old Black Man Becomes First in Louisiana to be Cured of Sickle Cell Disease
fake_endings: By Receiving a Bone Marrow Transplant

stories.title: A Bakery Where Vegan Isn’t a Compromise
stories.summary: Flour + Time in Atlanta is changing what vegan pastries can be. Founder Leah Bajalia starts with classic recipes, not vegan ones, to create croissants, biscuits, and doughnuts that taste just as indulgent as the originals — without any dairy or compromise.
stories.extended_summary: The flaky croissant at Flour + Time shatters with each bite, revealing an airy, buttery interior — with zero dairy. In Atlanta’s West Midtown, founder Leah Bajalia is rewriting the reputation of vegan baking. She doesn’t start with vegan recipes; she starts with classic French pastry, then adapts them using plant-based butter and careful temperature control. The result is cinnamon rolls, chive biscuits, and strawberry doughnuts that taste exactly as they should, proving plant-based is no longer a compromise.
stories.headline_split: A Bakery Where Vegan Isn’t

stories.title: A ballet company brings Asian folktales to the stage
stories.summary: The Asian American Ballet Project’s fourth annual concert, “Once Upon Our Time,” brings Asian folktales to the ballet stage — including a Vietnamese Cinderella story. Choreographer Lauren Huynh’s mother traveled to Vietnam for traditional costumes, making the production a family and cultural milestone.
stories.extended_summary: When dancer Lauren Huynh told her mom she was choreographing a ballet based on “Tấm Cám,” a Vietnamese Cinderella tale, her mom immediately booked a trip to Vietnam to select traditional clothing. That personal touch is at the heart of the Asian American Ballet Project’s upcoming concert, “Once Upon Our Time,” which features four ballets rooted in Asian folklore. For Huynh, it’s the first time she’s seen a Vietnamese story on stage — and a space where Asian American dancers are front and center.

stories.title: A major American milestone was the perfect time for Studyville founder Amanda Martin to write her first children’s book
stories.summary: Studyville founder Amanda Martin wrote 'Henry the Star-Spangled Spaniel' to teach civics to elementary students. The book, published in time for America's 250th birthday, follows a time-traveling dog who witnesses the signing of the Declaration of Independence. It's already kid-tested by 4,000 students.
stories.extended_summary: Amanda Martin never planned to write a children's book, but when her tutoring company needed early civics resources, she created a character that stuck. Henry the Star-Spangled Spaniel, based on her own Cavalier King Charles Spaniel, travels back in time in a red, white, and blue doghouse. With Simon & Schuster publishing it, the book brings history to life for young readers—and it's already a hit with thousands of kids.

stories.title: Adidas’ NIL pipeline is producing NBA rookies who already know the business
stories.summary: Mikel Brown Jr. entered the NBA with a head start: a multiyear NIL deal with Adidas he signed in high school. The six overall pick already knows the brand’s designers, has a sponsored AAU program, and brings a built-in audience of over 84,000 followers.
stories.extended_summary: Before he was drafted sixth overall by the Brooklyn Nets, Mikel Brown Jr. had already spent years embedded in Adidas’ ecosystem — meeting designers, learning the business, and building his own community. The 20-year-old guard signed an NIL deal with Adidas at 17 while in Overtime Elite, giving him a playbook most rookies lack. Now he arrives in the NBA with his own Adidas-sponsored AAU program, relationships with stars like Donovan Mitchell, and an audience that makes him a day-one asset for the brand.

stories.title: An idea worth wearing: UWM alum creates jewelry company based on an inclusive design
stories.summary: UWM alum Kayla Lokker turned a frustration with necklace clasps into Easy Wear Jewelry, featuring her patented Easy Clasp. The design was inspired by retractable ID holders and makes putting on necklaces effortless—a simple solution to an everyday problem.
stories.extended_summary: Kayla Lokker was always late because she couldn't fasten her necklace clasps fast enough. An entrepreneurship lesson urged her to solve everyday frustrations, and she created Easy Clasp—a mechanism adapted from retractable badge holders. Now her startup, Easy Wear Jewelry, offers necklaces that anyone can put on in seconds. Lokker's journey from a gymnast training 40 hours a week to a patent-holding founder shows how persistence pays off.

stories.title: Birmingham’s ‘Melanated Reader’ Crystal Forte is Leading a Movement One Black Book at a Time
stories.summary: Crystal Forte, the Birmingham-based @melanatedreader, launched the Reading Black Books Summer Reading Club with a goal of getting one million people to read a book by a Black author. She describes it as a mission to shift the culture of literacy.
stories.extended_summary: Crystal Forte wants one million people to read one Black-authored book this summer. The high school student support specialist and literary influencer started the Reading Black Books Summer Reading Club to tackle what she calls a literacy crisis. Her campaign is about more than numbers—it's about access, representation, and honoring elders like her grandmother, a Gee's Bend quilter. Forte is building community one recommendation at a time, and her online movement is growing fast.
stories.headline_split: Birmingham’s ‘Melanated Reader’ Crystal Forte is Leading a Movement

stories.title: Black Mom of 3 Launches Free New Scholarship Program to Help 25,000 Black Families Send Their Kids to College
stories.summary: After losing her mother at 16 and missing out on Spelman, India Brown created the Legacy Leaders Scholarship Program. It's free, has no essays or income limits, and aims to help 25,000 Black families earn guaranteed scholarship rewards for college.
stories.extended_summary: India Brown was accepted to Spelman College at 16, but after her mother died without life insurance, she couldn't afford to go. Decades later, she turned that heartbreak into action. The Legacy Leaders Scholarship Program gives families free access to one of the largest private college tuition reward networks, covering 440+ schools including HBCUs. No essays, no income checks—just a path to guaranteed scholarship dollars. Her goal: 25,000 Black families get college funding they can start earning years early.
stories.headline_split: Black Mom of 3 Launches Free New Scholarship Program

stories.title: Citizen of the Week: Natasha Agarwal
stories.summary: During lockdown, Natasha Agarwal learned that kids just 10 miles away lacked books. She started a book drive that collected 3,000 books, then founded Believe in Reading. Five years later, her nonprofit has distributed thousands of books and started literacy programs.
stories.extended_summary: Natasha Agarwal's bookshelves were full, but during the pandemic she discovered a 'book desert' just 10 miles away. Her first book drive for a local early childhood center pulled in 3,000 books. That one-time project turned into Believe in Reading, a youth-run nonprofit. Now a rising sophomore at Penn, Natasha has run multiple drives and started literacy programs serving low-income areas. In Philadelphia, where 71% of fourth graders read below grade level, her work is closing gaps one book at a time.

stories.extended_summary: Six months after earning his master’s in computer science, Divyanshu Verma is running a hedge fund with $4.3 million in capital and a proprietary AI research platform. The 23-year-old international student from India founded Verma Research Capital after serving as a quantitative portfolio manager in UWM’s Student Investment Club. His system, AEGIS, uses large language models to analyze earnings calls and company fundamentals. The fund has already returned 26% since launch, proving that a computer science background can be a powerful edge in finance.

stories.title: Confident Is This Head That Wears the Crown
stories.summary: Alhambra High grad Keiko Rakin is the first student from her district to join the Tournament of Roses Court. After advocating to expand eligibility boundaries, she now heads to Wellesley College, inspired by Hillary Clinton and Madeleine Albright.
stories.extended_summary: Keiko Rakin lived just outside the Rose Court eligibility zone—so she wrote a letter asking them to expand. They did. She became Alhambra Unified's first Rose Princess. But that's just one chapter. As student school board member and chair of the superintendent's advisory council, she's been breaking barriers for years. Now off to Wellesley, she's following in the footsteps of two secretaries of state. Her crown is confidence, and she's just getting started.

stories.title: Dallas Invented the Frozen Margarita, Here’s Where to Drink One This Weekend
stories.summary: The frozen margarita was invented in Dallas in 1971, when 25-year-old restaurateur Mariano Martinez turned a 7-Eleven Slurpee moment into a machine that changed cocktail culture. The original machine now sits in the Smithsonian — and the margaritas are still flowing in Dallas.
stories.extended_summary: In 1971, Mariano Martinez was a 25-year-old restaurant owner with a bartender ready to quit and 200 margarita orders a night. The solution came from a 7-Eleven Slurpee — itself a Dallas invention. He adapted a soft-serve ice cream machine to produce a consistent frozen margarita, and never patented it. Within years, the machine spread to 46 countries, and the margarita became America’s most popular cocktail. The original machine is now in the Smithsonian, but the best place to taste history is still at Mariano’s Hacienda in Dallas.
stories.headline_split: Dallas Invented the Frozen Margarita,

stories.title: Edmonds College student to represent U.S. in taekwondo at FISU America Games
stories.summary: Yusleny Rangel, a 19-year-old pre-nursing student at Edmonds College, will represent the U.S. in taekwondo at the 2026 FISU America Games. A third-degree black belt and two-time U17 national champion, she trains before dawn and balances school and family care.
stories.extended_summary: At 5 a.m., Yusleny Rangel is already at Master Cho’s Taekwondo in Lynnwood, training before her pre-nursing classes. The 19-year-old Edmonds College student just earned a spot on the U.S. National Collegiate Taekwondo Team for the 2026 FISU America Games. She’s the only woman from the Seattle area to qualify. After school, she helps care for her younger brother and assists in her mother’s housecleaning business. Rangel, a third-degree black belt who started taekwondo at age 5, says the sport gave her self-confidence and mental strength that carries through every part of her life.

stories.title: How People Without Degrees Are Snagging Corporate Jobs in the U.K.
stories.summary: Bruce Devlin was a carer for his mother from age 10 to 21, with no time to study or get a degree. Now 26, he’s a senior quality engineer, building his own tech tools and named a rising star at Scotland’s Digital Technology Awards — proving that passion can replace a diploma.
stories.extended_summary: Bruce Devlin spent 11 years as his mother’s carer, handling everything from shopping to medical appointments while scraping through school. He never earned a degree. Today, at 26, he’s a senior quality engineer at a software testing company, has built his own system outage monitoring tool, and was named a “rising star” at the ScotlandIS Digital Technology Awards. Devlin says his love for tinkering with computers and programming — and a determination to create — opened doors that traditional education didn’t. He now reinvests his salary into building video games and tech tools.
stories.headline_split: How People Without Degrees Are Snagging

stories.title: How a Grandson Took His 84-YO Grandpa’s Handwoven Sarees From Salem to the World
stories.summary: Praveen, 23, started Thatha’s Clothing to help his 84-year-old grandfather Chinnusami sell handwoven sarees directly to customers, bypassing middlemen. The Instagram page now supports 30 other weavers in Tamil Nadu, keeping a six-decade craft alive.
stories.extended_summary: Chinnusami, 84, has been weaving sarees for over 60 years in his village near Salem, Tamil Nadu. His hands tremble, but he refuses to stop. His grandson Praveen, 23, saw how middlemen took most of the profit and started Thatha’s Clothing on Instagram. Now, sarees from Chinnusami’s loom — and those of 30 other local weavers — reach customers across India and abroad. The page is named after the Tamil word for grandfather, and it’s transformed a fading craft into a sustainable livelihood.
stories.headline_split: How a Grandson Took

stories.title: How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in South China
stories.summary: Li Jiahe, a 24-year-old engineering student, never saw the spoon-billed sandpiper — but he helped save its habitat. After he and thousands of others campaigned online, China halted a highway that would have destroyed mudflats used by the critically endangered bird.
stories.extended_summary: The spoon-billed sandpiper is one of the rarest birds on Earth, with fewer than 500 individuals left. Li Jiahe, a 24-year-old engineering student, had never seen one, but when he learned a planned highway in Guangxi would cut through its wintering mudflats, he started an online campaign. Within weeks, thousands had emailed authorities, and on May 25, China’s central government halted the project. The provincial government has now promised to seek alternative routes and expert input. For Li, it’s proof that collective action — even from someone far away — can protect what’s most vulnerable.
stories.source_url: https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018593/How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in South China

stories.title: How two Minnesota teens saved a life with skills they never imagined using
stories.summary: Two 19-year-olds, Matias Vega and Eoin Porrata, used CPR training from a lifeguard course to save their friend's father after he collapsed on a pontoon boat. What started as a goofing-off trip turned into a life-or-death moment where their skills mattered.
stories.extended_summary: It was a beautiful July day on the Mississippi, and a group of friends had just returned from exploring an island carrying a big log. Then Luis Calderon suddenly slumped over in the pontoon. At first, Vega and Porrata thought it was a joke — but when his wife and daughter started crying, they realized it was real. The two 19-year-olds, who had just gotten CPR training for a lifeguard job, jumped into action. They performed chest compressions until paramedics arrived, and Calderon survived.

stories.title: Meet the Tree-Counting Juggernaut of New York City
stories.summary: Caleb Roenigk single-handedly surveyed 7,280 trees in New York City parks last year—more than 11% of all trees assessed. The volunteer tree-counter spent about 30 seconds per tree, making him the most prolific assessor in the city's deciennial census.
stories.extended_summary: In Bensonhurst Park, Caleb Roenigk approaches a London plane tree like he's sizing it for a suit. Tape measure out, phone ready—30 seconds later, he's onto the next. Between last summer and May, he repeated this ritual 7,280 times, far outrunning the second-place volunteer who clocked 4,689. Roenigk is responsible for over 11% of the 70,000 trees surveyed in NYC's parks. His dedication is giving the city an unmatched picture of its urban forest.
stories.headline_split: Meet the Tree-Counting

stories.title: Restoring Alabama: Meet the couple bringing the Black Belt prairie back to life
stories.summary: Alabama's Black Belt prairie is making a comeback, thanks to Jessica and Hunter Vick. This husband-and-wife team works with landowners to restore native grasslands, removing invasive cedars and conducting controlled burns. Their efforts are paying off: the eastern monarch butterfly population just jumped 64%. It's proof that restoring habitat, one property at a time, really works.
stories.extended_summary: Before talking about prescribed burns or cedar removal, Jessica and Hunter Vick burst with excitement: the eastern monarch butterfly population had increased 64% this year. For the couple behind Land Revival LLC, that news confirmed their mission matters. They help Alabama landowners restore native prairie—removing cedars, burning, and improving wildlife habitat. Hunter says it's about responsibility: 'What you own is not just about you. Land ownership is your responsibility to take care of it.' Their work is slowly bringing the Black Belt prairie back to life.
stories.headline_split: Meet the couple bringing the Black Belt prairie

stories.title: This New Lynwood Studio Is a Sober Living Sanctuary with Coffee, Tattoos, and Streetwear
stories.summary: Ruben Barcenas turned his sobriety into a community hub. High Channel Division in Lynwood is part coffee shop, part tattoo studio, part streetwear store—all designed to make sobriety attractive. 'People don't see the sober; they see the matcha,' he says. It's a space for healing that feels like a cool hangout.
stories.extended_summary: Ruben Barcenas wanted to make sobriety attractive, so he opened High Channel Division in Lynwood—a studio that blends coffee, tattoos, and streetwear. 'People don't see the sober; they see the matcha,' he says. The space started as a clothing store but grew into a cafe and private tattoo studio. Barcenas, who lost his father to intoxication, says the studio is about giving the community a chance to start brands and find wellness undercover. It's a sober sanctuary that feels like Zumiez for the hood.

stories.title: UW grad’s startup idea comes into focus with a digital marketplace for photographers
stories.summary: UW grad Sky Yang created SnapMatePhoto, a digital marketplace connecting people with affordable photographers. After friends kept asking him to take their graduation photos, he realized everyone deserves a nice portrait for life's big moments. The platform now lists over 120 photographers in Seattle, with rates starting at $35.
stories.extended_summary: Sky Yang was a senior at UW when friends started asking him to shoot their graduation photos—and offering to pay. He saw a gap: people couldn't afford professional photographers but still wanted quality portraits for important moments. So he built SnapMatePhoto, a digital marketplace connecting customers with photographers for graduations, weddings, and more. Now, over 120 photographers are listed in Seattle, with rates from $35 to $70. Yang's startup went through UW's accelerator and launched last October. 'Everyone deserves a nice graduation photo,' he says.

stories.title: When the heat doesn’t stop
stories.summary: In Chelsea, Massachusetts—one of the hottest cities in the state—climate justice organizer Jazmin Castellón is building a cooler future. She helped create Bear Park, a new green space with shade trees, and launched the TreeKeeper program, training 80 volunteers to care for over 200 trees. It's a frontline defense against extreme heat.
stories.extended_summary: Jazmin Castellón stands on Congress Street in Chelsea, looking at a new park she helped shape. When it opens this summer, it will offer shade in one of Massachusetts' hottest cities. Chelsea, densely populated with low-income and immigrant residents, has little tree cover and traps heat. Castellón's TreeKeeper program trains locals to care for and expand the urban canopy. Now 80 volunteers maintain over 200 trees. 'Those trees are crucial infrastructure,' she says—a frontline defense against extreme heat.

stories.title: A Tiny Mobile Bookshop Fosters a Novel Community in South Jersey
stories.summary: Charity Herndon's Austen's Shelf is a cozy mobile bookshop that brings whimsy to South Jersey. The 98-square-foot trailer features floral wallpaper, a fireplace mantel, and a Mr. Darcy cutout. Beyond selling books, she hosts audiobook walking clubs and craft pop-ups. Now she's opened a brick-and-mortar shop in Bordentown.
stories.extended_summary: Charity Herndon's mobile bookstore, Austen's Shelf, draws lines around the block. Inside the 98-square-foot trailer, visitors find floral wallpaper, a fireplace mantel, and a cardboard Mr. Darcy. Herndon, an author herself, stocks everything from Regency romance to thrillers. 'I always want people to find their whimsy,' she says. She also hosts book-themed outings like audiobook walking clubs and watch parties. After a year on the road, she opened a permanent shop in Bordentown, giving people even more space to linger.
stories.headline_split: A Tiny Mobile Bookshop Fosters a Novel

stories.title: A ‘Matchmaker’ Platform Boosts Farm Worker Incomes From Rs 6K to Rs 20K per Month in Nashik
stories.summary: A new AI-powered platform is helping farm workers in Nashik triple their incomes. Bharat Intelligence connects farmers with skilled laborers via WhatsApp, replacing chaotic morning trips to the labor chowk with reliable scheduling. Farmers like Bapusaheb Salunkhe now get workers on time, and laborers earn more—up from Rs 6,000 to Rs 20,000 a month.
stories.extended_summary: In Nashik, farmer Bapusaheb Salunkhe used to scramble for workers every morning. Now he taps his phone—a WhatsApp-based platform called Bharat Intelligence assigns a team instantly. The service uses AI to connect farmers with skilled laborers, replacing informal hiring systems. Co-founders Azaan Merchant and Gaurav Sanghai saw the labor problem as accepted but unsolved. Now workers' incomes have jumped from Rs 6,000 to Rs 20,000 per month, and farmers get reliable help. 'They come on time now,' Salunkhe says simply.
stories.headline_split: A ‘Matchmaker’ Platform Boosts Farm Worker Incomes

stories.title: Before El Niño Strikes, This IAS Officer Is Strengthening Gadchiroli’s Water Systems at Scale
stories.summary: IAS officer Avishyant Panda isn't waiting for El Niño to wreak havoc in Gadchiroli. He's leading a massive push to complete 4,600 water conservation works, 1,000 irrigation projects, and desilt key water bodies—all before a weaker monsoon hits. The goal: help villages store water now so they don't face crisis later.
stories.extended_summary: While others wait for the monsoon forecast, IAS officer Avishyant Panda is already fortifying Gadchiroli's water systems. With El Niño expected to bring a weaker monsoon, he's completed 4,600 water conservation works, 1,000 irrigation projects, and desilted key reservoirs. He's not waiting for a crisis—he's preparing the district in advance. 'Bracing for the possibility of a weaker monsoon, Panda has pushed for the completion of pending water conservation works,' the report says. His proactive approach is building resilience before any stress sets in.

stories.title: Bloop Museum takes you down a rabbit hole of early computers and vintage media
stories.summary: Bloop Museum in Penn Hills is a rabbit hole of early computers, vintage media, and arcade games. Founder Brendan Becker and 100 volunteers have restored around 25,000 objects, from vintage PCs to LaserDiscs. The museum turned a vacant municipal building into a nerdy paradise for the community.
stories.extended_summary: Bloop Museum is a computer history haven in a former Penn Hills municipal building. Founder Brendan Becker, a programmer and chiptune composer, spent 17 years collecting 25,000 objects—vintage PCs, arcade games, LaserDiscs. With 100 volunteers, he's restored and curated the collection. 'I noticed there are a lot of nerds here,' Becker says. The community has embraced it: school groups, enthusiasts, and even a few curmudgeons won over. It's a labor of love turning a vacant building into a treasure trove of tech history.
stories.headline_split: Bloop Museum takes you down a rabbit hole

stories.title: Hot Dog Harv Heads to NYC This Week

The 11-year-old went viral after performing 'HOTDOGS TO GO!' at his school talent show, and now he's singing before the big event on Coney Island.

Now, as Hot Dog Harv, he's headed to the Nathan's Famous Hot Dog Eating Contest on Coney Island to perform before the 40,000-strong crowd. Competitive eaters noticed him on Instagram and connected his family with Nathan's. The family is turning it into a New York trip with sightseeing and plays. stories.title: Move Over, Cronuts: This Denver Pastry Chef Is Making Baonuts
stories.summary: Denver pastry chef Eric Dale was stumped by doughnuts until he hit on the baonut—a deep-fried bao bun filled with creamy blueberry goodness. After months of trial, his sweet, airy creation is now a hit at Rioja's brunch. Move over cronuts: the baonut is here.
stories.extended_summary: Eric Dale, executive pastry chef at Denver's Rioja, spent months trying to perfect a brunch doughnut. Yeast dough was too finicky, cake doughnuts unsatisfying. Then came a breakthrough: deep-frying bao buns, the steamed Asian rolls. The result: the baonut—a sweet, airy sphere with creamy blueberry filling. 'We did a savory bao for Lunar New Year, and I wondered what it would be like to deep-fry them,' Dale says. After R&D, the baonut became a brunch staple. It's a playful twist that proves innovation often comes from unexpected places.

stories.title: Period tax scrapped in Pakistan: what the ruling means for women
stories.summary: Pakistan scrapped its sales tax on period products after a legal challenge by two young lawyers. The move recognizes menstruation as a health matter and aims to improve women's dignity and participation in society.
stories.extended_summary: Two lawyers, Mahnoor Omer and Ahsan Jehangir Khan, filed a court case and gathered thousands of signatures to challenge Pakistan's 18% sales tax on period products. Finance Minister Muhammad Aurangzeb announced the tax removal, calling sanitary products essential for women's health and dignity. While the price drop won't immediately reach all women, advocates say the most valuable impact is the public recognition that menstruation is a health issue, not a source of shame.

stories.fake_headline: New law in Rwanda eliminates VAT on sanitary products for rural women

stories.title: Photo Essay: More Paint
stories.summary: After nearly five years in prison, Vik Chopra started Washington's first prison podcast, 'Concrete Mama.' He now uses storytelling to empower justice-impacted voices and change the narrative around incarceration.
stories.extended_summary: Vik Chopra spent nearly five years at the Washington State Penitentiary, emerging with a strengthened sense of self and a mission to change how society views the justice-impacted community. He now runs 'Concrete Mama,' Washington’s first prison podcast, giving a platform to voices often silenced. Through his work, Chopra is transforming his own life and helping others find their voice after incarceration.

stories.title: Pojoaque Pueblo graduates celebrate sobriety on the ‘Path to Wellness’
stories.summary: Pojoaque Pueblo celebrated its first official graduation from the 'Path to Wellness' program, honoring two individuals who completed a year of substance use treatment. The program diverts nonviolent offenders toward behavioral health support instead of jail.
stories.extended_summary: A wax '1' sat atop a cake at Pojoaque Pueblo, marking Stefan Catanach's first year of sobriety and his graduation from the Path to Wellness program. The program, which has existed for over a decade, held its first official graduation ceremony for two graduates. It diverts people with nonviolent substance-related crimes toward a year of counseling, meetings, and job holding—an intense commitment that Chief Judge Anthony Mozingo says is only for those truly ready to change their lives.

stories.title: Secret Solvang tour guides show visitors and locals the best spots in town
stories.summary: Secret Solvang tours reveal hidden gems in a town often seen as a monolith of bakeries and windmills. Founder Wes Leslie now offers Viking tours for kids, giving them an immersive, crown-decorating adventure.
stories.extended_summary: Wes Leslie started Secret Solvang after realizing people saw his town as just bakeries and windmills. His tours dive deeper, featuring interviews with the mayor and quirky local businesses. In March, he launched Viking tours for kids ages 5–12, where they decorate crowns, visit the Viking Museum, and snack on aebleskiver—turning a tourist stop into an interactive discovery for families.

stories.title: She listened to her pupils. Four months later, a school was transformed
stories.summary: After asking students what they wanted to learn, headteacher Letizia Riddell overhauled Springburn Academy's curriculum in just four months. Students now earn qualifications and industry awards with college and industry partners.
stories.extended_summary: Letizia Riddell felt the traditional academic curriculum wasn't working for her students at Springburn Academy. In February, she asked them directly what careers they wanted and what courses would help. By June, she had transformed the school's offerings, bringing in college lecturers and industry partners so students could earn vocational qualifications. 'Foot on the gas, don’t take no for an answer,' she said, proving that school reform doesn't have to wait for national policy.

stories.title: Skyline Pastures: A multi-species farm built from scratch
stories.summary: While deployed in Kuwait, Army National Guardsman CJ Lafferty wrote a business plan for a livestock farm. Six years later, he runs a multi-species, pasture-based operation in Pennsylvania—all while holding down a full-time military job.
stories.extended_summary: CJ Lafferty had been raising a few hens and goats in his suburban backyard when he was deployed to Kuwait. With downtime on his hands, he wrote a livestock business plan. After being outbid on several properties, he and his wife bought a 12-acre farm in Mohrsville, PA, sight unseen. Over six years, Lafferty built a multi-species farm with broilers, pigs, and more—learning everything from YouTube and Joel Salatin, all while serving full-time in the National Guard.

stories.title: Training the Young Workers Chicago’s Schools Are Leaving Behind
stories.summary: Manufacturing Connect helps Chicago youth gain skills and certifications for well-paying manufacturing jobs. Torres Hughes, a former participant who now speaks at graduations, credits the program for turning his life around.
stories.extended_summary: Torres Hughes stood in front of 10 Manufacturing Connect graduates, remembering his own uncertain start as a kid from Austin who didn't think he was college material. He graduated the program in 2012, started work the next Monday, and now makes good money with skills he can take anywhere. In the audience, 17-year-old Artis Wash was already planning his next certification. These are the young people that Manufacturing Renaissance serves—giving them a foothold in an industry that often overlooks them.

stories.title: 'They left me no other option': Reedley farmer gives 125,000+ pounds of nectarines away amid lawsuit
stories.summary: After being sued by a large ag corporation for allegedly breaking a contract, Reedley farmer Cesar Mora decided to give away over 125,000 pounds of nectarines for free. Thousands of people have already shown up to collect the fruit, turning a legal dispute into a community windfall.
stories.extended_summary: Cesar Mora, a third-generation farmer, was sued by Giumarra Brothers Fruit Company for sending his nectarines to another buyer. Instead of letting the crop go to waste, he posted videos on Instagram and started giving them away. The response was overwhelming—over 866,000 views and lines of people with buckets. Mora says the corporation left him no choice, but the community is reaping the sweetness of his defiance.
stories.headline_split: 'They left me no other option': Reedley farmer gives 125,000+ pounds of nectarines away
stories.fake_headline: Teen Inventor Creates Edible Water Bottles From Seaweed, Wins $50,000 Prize

stories.title: From prison tablets to Cleveland kitchens: How EDWINS has expanded its culinary training to nearly every state
stories.summary: Drew Hanchett found a culinary curriculum on his tablet in a county jail in Arizona. After completing it, he was flown to Cleveland for in-person training at EDWINS Leadership & Restaurant Institute. The program, which started with tablet courses in prisons, has expanded to nearly every state and offers a path out of incarceration.
stories.extended_summary: Behind bars in Tucson, Drew Hanchett devoured videos on knife skills and butchery through Edovo, an education app on prison tablets. He didn't know the courses were from EDWINS, a Cleveland program that flies graduates out for hands-on training. EDWINS founder Brandon Chrostowski, once arrested as a teen, built the institute to prove culinary training can break the cycle of incarceration. The Cleveland Browns sponsor flights for graduates. Hanchett now works in a professional kitchen—a journey that started with a tablet in a cell.
stories.headline_split: From prison tablets to Cleveland kitchens: How EDWINS has
fake_endings: created a culinary school in every state
trained inmates to become professional chefs

stories.title: Students pursue University-funded independent research projects over summer
stories.summary: Northwestern student Alice Chilton is using a Summer Undergraduate Research Grant to study the history of Britain's Red Ladder Theatre Company in archives across England. The grant program has expanded to include more humanities students earlier in their studies.
stories.extended_summary: Rising sophomore Alice Chilton will spend her summer in England, not on vacation but poring over play scripts, production photos, and stage management notes in archives. She's one of 301 Northwestern students awarded a Summer Undergraduate Research Grant. The program, which once favored STEM juniors, now encourages first- and second-year students from all disciplines to pursue independent projects. For Chilton, it's a chance to be her own boss and dive into radical socialist theater history.

stories.fake_headline: High school students get paid to study local rivers in environmental internship

stories.title: The ’70s: Teen Made His Own Backstage Passes and They Worked
stories.summary: In the 1970s, teen Mike Young made his own backstage passes using a photo booth and stick-on letters. They worked, getting him upgrades and access to concerts—a hilarious tale of ingenuity and guts.
stories.extended_summary: Mike Young wanted backstage access to concerts but didn't know anyone in the music business. So as a high school kid, he made his own passes. He photographed real passes, took a headshot in a mall photo booth, bought stick-on letters and laminate, and created passes that looked official. He and his girlfriend would dress up, flash the passes, and often get seat upgrades. His story reads like a Cameron Crowe movie—except it actually happened.

stories.title: Yellow Means Creepy: The Color-Coded Anti-Harassment System Used by Restaurant Workers
stories.summary: Chef Erin Wade created a simple color-coded system to combat sexual harassment at her restaurant Homeroom. The system empowers staff to signal problem customers without confrontation, and it's now a model for the industry.
stories.extended_summary: When Chef Erin Wade learned that nearly all her staff had faced sexual harassment—including a server whose blouse was reached under by a father of four—she knew training alone wouldn't work. So she and her team created a three-step color-coded system: green for good, yellow for creepy, and red for immediate intervention. Staff can discreetly signal a customer's behavior, and managers respond accordingly. The system has been so effective it's being adopted by other restaurants, offering a practical solution to an industry-wide problem.
